title: Renting a Boat in Rhodes and the Dodecanese: A Practical Guide
topic: Renting a boat in Rhodes and the Dodecanese
html:
Strung along the south-eastern edge of the Aegean, the Dodecanese is a scatter of islands where medieval castles, volcanic coves and turquoise shallows sit within easy reach of one another. Rhodes anchors the group as its largest and busiest island, but the real reward of being on the water here is how quickly you can slip away from the crowds. A short crossing takes you from a buzzing harbour to a silent bay where the only sound is the slap of water against the hull. For travellers who want to set their own pace, the simplest way to begin is to rent a boat and let the islands unfold one anchorage at a time.

Why the Dodecanese suits a boat trip

The Dodecanese rewards curiosity. The islands are close together, so you are rarely more than a couple of hours from your next stop, yet each has a distinct character. Symi is famous for its pastel neoclassical houses stacked around a deep harbour. Tilos is quiet and wild, with empty beaches and walking trails. Nisyros hides a steaming volcanic crater you can walk into. Halki feels frozen in a gentler decade. Having your own vessel turns these into a loose itinerary rather than a fixed ferry timetable, and you can linger wherever the light or the swimming happens to be best.

Crucially, the short hops mean you spend more time swimming and exploring and less time covering open water. That makes the region forgiving for those still building confidence at the helm, while still offering enough variety to keep experienced sailors happy across a full week.

Choosing the right vessel

What you hire should follow what you want from the trip. A few honest questions help narrow the field.

  • Day on the water: a small motorboat or RIB is ideal for reaching nearby beaches around Rhodes, Lindos or the channel toward Symi.
  • A relaxed week: a sailing yacht lets you island-hop slowly, harnessing the steady afternoon breeze that builds across the Aegean.
  • Family or group comfort: a catamaran gives shade, stability and deck space, which matters when children or non-swimmers are aboard.
  • Speed and range: a motor yacht covers ground quickly if you want to fit several islands into a tight schedule.

If you are unsure, comparing options side by side makes the trade-offs obvious. Browsing a marketplace where you can filter by size, cabins and home port is the fastest way to picture your trip. Take a moment to browse boats before committing, and read what previous guests say about each one.

Skippered or bareboat

The Aegean has a reputation for the meltemi, a strong northerly wind that can blow for days in high summer. It makes for thrilling sailing but demands respect, particularly around exposed headlands and in tight harbours. If you hold the relevant qualifications and have handled a yacht in fresh conditions, a bareboat charter gives you complete freedom. If not, hiring a skipper is money well spent. A good local skipper knows which bays stay calm when the wind gets up, where to find a sheltered berth, and how to read the forecast for the days ahead.

A skipper is not a luxury in the Dodecanese, it is local knowledge you cannot download. They turn a windy week into a memorable one.

Many first-timers split the difference: a skipper for the first day or two to learn the ropes, then more independence once they feel settled. When you hire a boat through a marketplace, you can usually add a skipper as an option rather than hunting for one separately.

Planning a sample route

A relaxed week from Rhodes might run like this, weather permitting.

  1. Day one: leave Rhodes and cross to Symi for dinner in the harbour.
  2. Day two: swim at Agios Georgios bay, then sail to Tilos.
  3. Day three: explore Tilos on foot before pushing on to Nisyros.
  4. Day four: walk the volcano, then anchor off Kos for the evening.
  5. Days five and six: drift back via Halki and quiet coves toward Rhodes.

Treat any plan as a sketch. Wind and mood will redraw it, and that is part of the pleasure.

Practical tips before you go

Book earlier for July and August, when the best vessels are snapped up months ahead. Check exactly what is included, from fuel policy and dinghy to bedding and a final clean. Pack soft bags rather than hard cases, reef-safe sun cream and shoes that grip a wet deck. Always confirm the cancellation terms and whether a deposit covers the excess. Comparing listings transparently helps you avoid surprises, so it pays to find a boat with clear pricing and verified reviews.

Owners welcome too

If you keep a boat in the Dodecanese and it sits idle for weeks each season, it can earn its keep while you are ashore. Reaching travellers worldwide is straightforward when you list your boat on a global platform that handles enquiries and bookings for you. However you come to the water here, the islands are best met slowly, with salt on your skin and the next anchorage already in sight.
